topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The bright, almost offensively cheerful arrangement sat on my worn kitchen table, a riot of pink roses and sunny yellow lilies. I stared at them, the silence in the apartment amplifying the dull thrumming behind my ribs. There was no note, no whisper explaining the gesture, just the heavy scent of cut petals. My fingers traced the velvety texture of a deep crimson bloom, a strange, hollow feeling settling in my chest. I picked up a nearby mug, the ceramic cool against my palm, and watched the light catch the dew on a leaf. I didn't move to touch the flowers, just let the weight of them settle, a conspicuous splash of vibrancy against the muted beige of my existence.

[story 2]

I found the bouquet tucked into the entryway, abandoned on the welcome mat. They were vibrant, too vibrant, demanding attention in a space that felt perpetually shrouded in shadow. A tightness constricted my throat as I took them, the stems cool and slick beneath my fingertips. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to let a sliver of the gray afternoon seep in. My gaze drifted over the blossoms, a flicker of something unfamiliar—a sharp, unexpected pang—before I quickly averted my eyes, focusing instead on the chipped paint on the windowsill. Why this display? It felt like an imposition, an unnecessary flourish in a life that felt stubbornly flat.

[story 3]

When the delivery person left the flowers on my doorstep, I didn't immediately reach for them. I stood by the door, my shoulders hunching slightly as I watched the vehicle disappear around the corner. The flowers themselves were undeniably lovely, a cascade of impossible color. A low, tight sound escaped my lips—more of a sigh than a word—as I finally lifted the arrangement. The weight of the stems felt burdensome, a physical manifestation of the complicated, churning stillness inside me. I carried them into the living room, placing them carefully on the mantelpiece, then immediately turning away, seeking the familiar comfort of the darkened hallway.
